category_summary:
The AI community was gripped by a **NeurIPS 2026** [official apology](/?date=2026-03-28&category=social#item-806c8985abf6) over an overly broad US sanctions compliance link in their conference handbook, a rare institutional controversy drawing sharp scrutiny from the global ML research community.

- **Sam Altman** shared a [viral story](/?date=2026-03-28&category=social#item-56b4f52db2d5) (1.27M views) of someone using **ChatGPT** to design an mRNA vaccine protocol that saved their dog, while also announcing the **Stargate** Michigan data center's [first steel beams](/?date=2026-03-28&category=social#item-556ebdbd54a9) going up with **Oracle**
- **Matt Shumer** reported that **Anthropic** has trained a model 'dramatically' smarter than **Claude Opus 4.6**, generating intense speculation about next-generation frontier capabilities
- **Google AI** [recapped a packed week](/?date=2026-03-28&category=social#item-003110c00d4b) including **Gemini 3.1 Flash Live** launch and **Lyria 3 Pro** for music generation
- **Greg Brockman** announced plugins are now available in **OpenAI Codex**, extending its developer tooling capabilities

Open-source momentum continued as **Clement Delangue** outlined **HuggingFace's** [roadmap for enabling](/?date=2026-03-28&category=social#item-59671d8f10a0) 3M+ models via inference providers and called for more open agent traces datasets. **Allen AI** fully [open-sourced **MolmoBot**](/?date=2026-03-28&category=social#item-5d24d3074004), a robotic manipulation suite trained entirely in simulation. **François Chollet** [argued that true AGI](/?date=2026-03-28&category=social#item-e3ab0afc4b84) must create its own problem-solving harnesses, connecting **ARC-AGI-3** benchmarks to [real scientific reasoning gaps](/?date=2026-03-28&category=social#item-d853bcddf9a2). Meanwhile, **Bret Taylor's Sierra** expanded internationally by [acquiring **Opera Tech**](/?date=2026-03-28&category=social#item-1e27bbb05c9a) in Japan.

items.title: We want to speak directly to the concern many of you have expressed, and we owe you a clear explanat...
items.content:
We want to speak directly to the concern many of you have expressed, and we owe you a clear explanation of what happened, why it happened, and where we stand now. We understand this situation caused genuine alarm and we take that seriously.

In preparing the NeurIPS 2026 handbook, we included a link to a US government sanctions tool that covers a significantly broader set of restrictions than those NeurIPS is actually required to follow. This error was due to miscommunication between the NeurIPS Foundation and our legal team; there was never an intention to restrict participation beyond our mandatory compliance obligations. The responsibility for that error is ours as an organization, and we deeply apologize for the alarm and impact this miscommunication had on our community.

We have updated the link and clarified the text of our policy, which is consistent with that of ACM and IEEE, as well as other international conferences and NeurIPS in the past. As in previous years, NeurIPS welcomes submissions from all compliant institutions and individuals.

We want to reiterate that NeurIPS is a community-driven event, created by and for the community, and strives to be inclusive. The NeurIPS 2026 organizing committee was particularly saddened to learn of this institutional miscommunication. The organizing committee has taken on the responsibility of running the conference this year with the goal of fostering open communication, knowledge sharing, and global scientific discourse.

We thank the community for bringing this issue to our attention and working with us through this situation.

items.title: The coolest meeting I had this week with was Paul, who used ChatGPT and other LLMs to create an mRNA...
items.content:
The coolest meeting I had this week with was Paul, who used ChatGPT and other LLMs to create an mRNA vaccine protocol to save his dog Rosie. It is amazing story.

"The chat bots empowered me as an individual to act with the power of a research institute - planning, education, troubleshooting, compliance, and yes, real scientific design work in converting genomic data to a vaccine prescription and designing the treatment protocol around it. But they worked alongside humans at every step. The combination is what made it possible."

It immediately got me thinking "this should be a company".

Also, Paul is an extraordinary guy. This should be easy to do, but it is not yet.

items.title:
Next steps:
- enable the 50,000 models available in inference providers
- enable the 3,000,000 model...
items.content:
Next steps:
- enable the 50,000 models available in inference providers
- enable the 3,000,000 models available on HF
- local free fast inference with llama.cpp
- train and bring your own model!

We don't want a world where you're forced to choose between two or three lookalike models with the same biases, limitations, forced to pay fortunes in tokens even for small tasks and send all your data to the cloud. 

We want a world where you have real model choice, options and freedom for your agents. Cloud, local, small, big, specialized, general, English or French, fast or slow, from six months ago or from six seconds ago, from third party or your own!

Let's go!

Next steps:

  • enable the 50,000 models available in inference providers

  • train and bring your own model!

We don't want a world where you're forced to choose between two or three lookalike models with the same biases, limitations, forced to pay fortunes in tokens even for small tasks and send all your data to the cloud.

We want a world where you have real model choice, options and freedom for your agents. Cloud, local, small, big, specialized, general, English or French, fast or slow, from six months ago or from six seconds ago, from third party or your own!

Let's go!

items.title:
Here’s a recap of everything we released this week:

— Gemini 3.1 Flash Live, delivering our highest...
items.content:
Here’s a recap of everything we released this week:

— Gemini 3.1 Flash Live, delivering our highest-quality audio experience yet with improved reasoning and latency for more natural voice interactions

— An update to @GeminiApp on desktop that enables you to seamlessly transition from other AI apps by importing your preferences and chat history in just a few clicks

— Lyria 3 Pro, supporting high-fidelity music tracks up to 3 minutes long with the ability to prompt for structural elements like intros, verses, and bridges

— A suite of new Gemini features on Google TV, such as narrated deep dives on educational topics and real-time sports overviews

— Google Translate’s Live translate with headphones, now on iOS and expanding to more countries for real-time translation across 70+ languages

— A new research partnership between @GoogleDeepMind and Agile Robots to develop the next generation of helpful robots

items.title: AGI will make its own harness (or whatever else it needs to solve a new problem). As long as you nee...
items.content:
AGI will make its own harness (or whatever else it needs to solve a new problem). As long as you need a human engineer to handcraft a task-specific harness/system for each new problem, AI isn't general. It's an automation tool to be wielded by software engineers.

Harness-related research is important and valuable -- as a vector of better automation. But I don't think it gets us closer to general intelligence. General intelligence is when you can adapt on your own.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet argues AGI will create its own problem-solving harnesses without human engineering. Until AI can adapt on its own without task-specific human-crafted systems, it's automation tooling, not general intelligence.
items.reasoning: Foundational philosophical argument about AGI from ARC-AGI creator with very high engagement (96K views). Defines a clear criterion for distinguishing automation from AGI.
items.summary_html: Chollet argues AGI will create its own problem-solving harnesses without human engineering. Until AI can adapt on its own without task-specific human-crafted systems, it's automation tooling, not general intelligence.

Been really cool to see the traction of @NousResearch Hermes Agent, the open source agent that grows with you! 

Hermes Agent is open-source and remembers what it learns and gets more capable over time, with a multi-level memory system and persistent dedicated machine access.

Starting today, you can use a bunch of @huggingface open-source models thanks to our inference provider partners.

Let's go open agents!

items.title: Many people expect that current AI is ready to cure cancer and do breakthrough new science. ARC-AGI-...
items.content:
Many people expect that current AI is ready to cure cancer and do breakthrough new science. ARC-AGI-3 envs are like a microcosm of the scientific method: you must observe a tiny world, form a theory of how it works, test it, iterate until correct. Over the course of a few minutes.

If AI can't do it in an ultra-simple, ultra-small scale setting that is explicitly designed to be as accessible as possible, I expect there are a few steps missing until AI can crack the nature of reality.

items.title:
Been playing around with @openclaw. 

The big news. I don't have a Mac Mini or a DGX computer. 

So ...
items.content:
Been playing around with @openclaw. 

The big news. I don't have a Mac Mini or a DGX computer. 

So I set up one at https://t.co/jeP0nebHIv. 

Runs in the cloud. 

Is secure. 

Plus they have a curated Skill Hub where you can install skills in one click with zero setup cost. 

And, yeah, I know I can't use Apple's iMessage like the cool kids, but then I didn't need to put down $900 for a Mac Mini either. :-)

Lots of you are still setting up an OpenClaw, I saw that myself where people waited in line at NVIDIA GTC to buy a DGX and get it loaded with OpenClaw or NVIDIA's enterprise variant, NemoClaw. And those people are AI professionals, not everyday people.

After I got home from GTC I wanted one too to keep up, but don't have the $5,000 to buy an NVIDIA DGX, or $900 to buy a Mac Mini. 

Plus I am in a group that is still just trying to learn about what I might want to do with one, and hear about all the security issues about setting them up on a machine that has access to your life. 

Luckily there are easier to afford, and safer, approaches. https://t.co/jeP0nebHIv is one. Costs $20 a month. 

Can be cancelled anytime. 

Runs in its own sandbox on a cloud server. They also have a curated Skill Hub — think of it like an app store for your Claw — where you can install skills in one click and it costs you nothing to install. 

No configuration, no debugging, just click and go. 

Yeah, that misses some of the special sauce of OpenClaw. 

Running it on a Mac Mini lets you add your own services that you control on your own machine, and hook it into, say, Apple's Messenger so that your Claw can send messages to your friends and coworkers.

 But many people aren't ready for all that yet, they just want to learn and having a cloud setup makes a lot of sense until you are ready to put down $1,000 to $5,000 (or more, @AlexFinn has a $30,000 setup of three Apple Mac Studios) and MyClaw fits the bill and is awesome.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer provides a detailed walkthrough of setting up OpenClaw via MyClaw cloud service as an affordable alternative to running it locally on Mac Mini or NVIDIA DGX. Discusses pricing ($20/mo cloud vs $900-$5000+ hardware), security benefits of sandboxed cloud, curated Skill Hub, and trade-offs vs local setup.
items.reasoning: This is the most substantive post in the batch. Scobleizer provides practical, detailed information about the OpenClaw/NemoClaw agentic AI ecosystem. Reveals that OpenClaw was a major presence at NVIDIA GTC with people buying DGX hardware to run it. Shows the agentic AI ecosystem is maturing with cloud hosting options, skill marketplaces, and tiered access points. Very high engagement (85K views, 195 likes). Key ecosystem data: OpenClaw runs on Mac Mini, DGX, or cloud; NVIDIA's enterprise variant is NemoClaw; MyClaw is a cloud hosting service.

Plus they have a curated Skill Hub where you can install skills in one click with zero setup cost.

And, yeah, I know I can't use Apple's iMessage like the cool kids, but then I didn't need to put down $900 for a Mac Mini either. :-)

Lots of you are still setting up an OpenClaw, I saw that myself where people waited in line at NVIDIA GTC to buy a DGX and get it loaded with OpenClaw or NVIDIA's enterprise variant, NemoClaw. And those people are AI professionals, not everyday people.

After I got home from GTC I wanted one too to keep up, but don't have the $5,000 to buy an NVIDIA DGX, or $900 to buy a Mac Mini.

Plus I am in a group that is still just trying to learn about what I might want to do with one, and hear about all the security issues about setting them up on a machine that has access to your life.

Luckily there are easier to afford, and safer, approaches. https://t.co/jeP0nebHIv is one. Costs $20 a month.

Can be cancelled anytime.

Runs in its own sandbox on a cloud server. They also have a curated Skill Hub — think of it like an app store for your Claw — where you can install skills in one click and it costs you nothing to install.

No configuration, no debugging, just click and go.

Yeah, that misses some of the special sauce of OpenClaw.

Running it on a Mac Mini lets you add your own services that you control on your own machine, and hook it into, say, Apple's Messenger so that your Claw can send messages to your friends and coworkers.

But many people aren't ready for all that yet, they just want to learn and having a cloud setup makes a lot of sense until you are ready to put down $1,000 to $5,000 (or more, @AlexFinn has a $30,000 setup of three Apple Mac Studios) and MyClaw fits the bill and is awesome.

items.title: @jackclarkSF Back then just after the bloom days, @Thom_Wolf hired the team behind the falcon models...
items.content:
@jackclarkSF Back then just after the bloom days, @Thom_Wolf hired the team behind the falcon models who were at the frontier at the time to work on this and got a big cluster from AWS for it but then they got scared by competition and didn't feel like it was central to HF if I remember correctly and left to start their own startup which was a bummer for us to be honest and led us to focus more on small models which lots of impactful work there in my opinion.

However, maybe now is a good time to start again in case you know people who are good at training who would be excited to join, happy to explore!

items.title: I don't think the solution is going to be Visa and Mastercard, which were built for humans at cash r...
items.content:
I don't think the solution is going to be Visa and Mastercard, which were built for humans at cash registers. They're too expensive for the kind of rapid-fire small-scale transactions we're going to see with agents ($0.3 + 3%). They're also quite slow (T+48h settlement)

So you need a solution natively built for this.

items.title:
Advertising in AI gets a BAD rep.

But at least one AI service provider seems to be handling it well...
items.content:
Advertising in AI gets a BAD rep.

But at least one AI service provider seems to be handling it well. 

Spoke last week with a cancer doctor about AI and specifically about the “ChatGPT for doctors” tool, Open Evidence - he said he loves the platform. 

He also loves that it’s free. 

And it’s free because of ads. 

“I think the ads on Open Evidence are a fair price to pay for that service,” he said. 

“And, quite frankly, I never have clicked on one (not out of any principle, just haven’t … yet),” he added. 

The doctor said it has completely changed the way he works (and even sometimes will ask questions to the AI *with* the patient and validate the output and talk through it with them). 

Two example convos below. 

Obviously need to watch out for overreach from pharma and will be interesting to see what the new head of ads at OpenAI will do (hi Dave), but the doctor is really happy with the product experience.

Definitely one to watch.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie K. Miller shares a cancer doctor's positive experience with Open Evidence, an ad-supported 'ChatGPT for doctors' tool, discussing how advertising in AI can work well when done right.
items.reasoning: Original first-hand reporting on real-world AI adoption in healthcare. Touches on important AI business model question (ads in AI). Allie Miller is a well-known AI commentator. Relevant to OpenAI's new ads head hire.
items.summary_html: Allie K. Miller shares a cancer doctor's positive experience with Open Evidence, an ad-supported 'ChatGPT for doctors' tool, discussing how advertising in AI can work well when done right.

items.title:
Both CLI and MCP are useful.

They can coexist and complement each other.

Here are just a few ways ...
items.content:
Both CLI and MCP are useful.

They can coexist and complement each other.

Here are just a few ways an MCP server is better than a CLI:

• Bidirectional communication: you get a persistent connection with MCP where the server can push updates, stream progress, or notify the agent.

• Elicitation: MCP servers can ask the model (or user) for clarification mid-execution.

• Scoped permissions: built-in OAuth and permission boundaries. No need to sandbox a shell to prevent malicious commands.

• Enterprise governance: audit trails, auth handshakes, and multi-tenant authorization out of the box.

• Remote execution: MCP over streamable HTTP means agents can use complex backends without local installs.

• Stateful by default: You get session management, connection pooling, and multi-step workflows much simpler than using a CLI.

I hope more companies embrace both.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino provides a detailed comparison of CLI vs MCP for AI agents, listing 6 advantages of MCP (bidirectional comms, elicitation, scoped permissions, enterprise governance, remote execution, stateful sessions) while arguing both should coexist. 14K views.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical analysis of a hot debate in AI tooling. Well-structured comparison with specific technical points. Relevant to how AI agents interact with tools.
items.summary_html: Svpino provides a detailed comparison of CLI vs MCP for AI agents, listing 6 advantages of MCP (bidirectional comms, elicitation, scoped permissions, enterprise governance, remote execution, stateful sessions) while arguing both should coexist. 14K views.
items.content_html:
Both CLI and MCP are useful.

They can coexist and complement each other.

Here are just a few ways an MCP server is better than a CLI:

• Bidirectional communication: you get a persistent connection with MCP where the server can push updates, stream progress, or notify the agent.

• Elicitation: MCP servers can ask the model (or user) for clarification mid-execution.

• Scoped permissions: built-in OAuth and permission boundaries. No need to sandbox a shell to prevent malicious commands.

• Enterprise governance: audit trails, auth handshakes, and multi-tenant authorization out of the box.

• Remote execution: MCP over streamable HTTP means agents can use complex backends without local installs.

• Stateful by default: You get session management, connection pooling, and multi-step workflows much simpler than using a CLI.

I hope more companies embrace both.

items.title: @stanislavfort Flat solutions tend to generalize better because they don't need to be represented wi...
items.content: @stanislavfort Flat solutions tend to generalize better because they don't need to be represented with as much precision (more compressible), larger margins (linearization), better Occam factors, better bounds. Not invariant, not the only factor, but also not a spurious empirical association.
items.author: @andrewgwils
items.summary: Andrew Gordon Wilson explains why flat solutions generalize better in ML — compressibility, larger margins, better Occam factors. Technical response to @stanislavfort.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical insight from a leading ML researcher on generalization theory. Low engagement but high technical value.

items.title:
A new repo just gave your Claude Code instances the ability to find each other and talk!

Claude Cod...
items.content:
A new repo just gave your Claude Code instances the ability to find each other and talk!

Claude Code already has built-in agent teams. But those require pre-planning. 

You decide upfront who talks to whom.

This open-source tool works differently. 

Sessions you started independently can discover each other on their own. No setup needed. 

One instance notices overlapping work. It reaches out to coordinate. Spontaneously. 

A lightweight broker runs on localhost with SQLite. 

Each session registers through an MCP server. Messages arrive instantly via channel push.

Here's what each instance can do:
> Find all other running sessions
> Send messages to any peer
> Share a summary of current work
> Auto-generate context from git state

Fully local. No cloud. 

One git clone to install.

Fully local. No cloud.

One git clone to install.

items.title:
When Claude shows me a three-page-long plan for a fix, I have two options:

1. Read it, confirm that...
items.content:
When Claude shows me a three-page-long plan for a fix, I have two options:

1. Read it, confirm that it's all correct, and have it implemented wrong anyway.

2. Not read it, confirm it's all correct, and have it implemented wrong anyway.

I prefer to choose option 2, then test, and then tell Claude that it implemented it wrong. How about you?
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov humorously describes the futile experience of reviewing Claude's detailed plans — whether you read them or not, implementation will be wrong anyway. He prefers skipping the review and just testing.
items.reasoning: Relatable and widely-engaged observation (169 likes, 24K views, 94 replies) about the practical experience of using Claude for coding. Captures a common pain point in LLM-assisted development.
items.summary_html: Burkov humorously describes the futile experience of reviewing Claude's detailed plans — whether you read them or not, implementation will be wrong anyway. He prefers skipping the review and just testing.
items.content_html:
When Claude shows me a three-page-long plan for a fix, I have two options:

1. Read it, confirm that it's all correct, and have it implemented wrong anyway.

2. Not read it, confirm it's all correct, and have it implemented wrong anyway.

I prefer to choose option 2, then test, and then tell Claude that it implemented it wrong. How about you?

items.title: It helps to think of ARC-AGI-3 as a different test entirely than the previous ARC-AGIs. It measures ...
items.content:
It helps to think of ARC-AGI-3 as a different test entirely than the previous ARC-AGIs. It measures different things (though, as in the previous tests, precisely what it measures isn’t clear) and has different rules.

That doesn’t mean it isn’t good, but it is its own thing.

items.title:
Dreamina Seedance 2.0 is by far the best video model I've tried, and Dreamina makes it even better.
...
items.content:
Dreamina Seedance 2.0 is by far the best video model I've tried, and Dreamina makes it even better.

You can try it in the link below.

It's not just about the quality of the output, but about how much control you have over your video's look.

Watch my video here.

I'm attaching reference images and asking the model to generate a video using them. I can reference each image using the @ symbol to instruct the model which image to use.

You can even upload a clip and use its camera movement, styles from an image, and audio vibe from a track.

By the way, you can take an existing video and replace, remove, or add elements to it while the model preserves everything else.

This is the closest we've gotten to "editing videos like photos".
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino provides a detailed review of Dreamina Seedance 2.0, calling it the best video model he's tried. Highlights reference image control, camera movement transfer, style transfer, and video editing capabilities. 30K views.

Dreamina Seedance 2.0 is by far the best video model I've tried, and Dreamina makes it even better.

You can try it in the link below.

It's not just about the quality of the output, but about how much control you have over your video's look.

Watch my video here.

I'm attaching reference images and asking the model to generate a video using them. I can reference each image using the @ symbol to instruct the model which image to use.

You can even upload a clip and use its camera movement, styles from an image, and audio vibe from a track.

By the way, you can take an existing video and replace, remove, or add elements to it while the model preserves everything else.

This is the closest we've gotten to "editing videos like photos".

items.title: @congressdj Long ago I interviewed Ford's head of safety. He told me that in accidents 80% of humans...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer provides detailed argument for Tesla FSD safety, citing Ford's safety research that 80% of humans don't do the right thing in accidents, and how FSD applies full braking to minimize impact energy.
items.reasoning: Most substantive post in the FSD safety thread. High engagement (154 likes, 11K views). Cites specific data points and makes clear safety argument.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer provides detailed argument for Tesla FSD safety, citing Ford's safety research that 80% of humans don't do the right thing in accidents, and how FSD applies full braking to minimize impact energy.
items.content_html:
@congressdj Long ago I interviewed Ford's head of safety. He told me that in accidents 80% of humans did not do the right thing. Even if an accident is unavoidable the right thing to do is to apply full braking to scrub off as much energy from the car so that an accident is more survivable.

You can see here that your Tesla is doing exactly that. It sees a potential accident that would be very hard to avoid (let's say the bus actually fully ran the light). Hitting it at 10 mph is far more survivable than hitting it at 35. (I once saw a woman get killed in a 35 mph accident so it does happen).

This is just one more reason why I believe Tesla's using FSD are far safer than human driven cars. Even my eight year old Tesla does this.

items.title: @FakePsyho Having played all of these games, I feel strongly that I would have scored >95% in a real...
items.content:
@FakePsyho Having played all of these games, I feel strongly that I would have scored >95% in a real testing session. Even the #1 human tester replay tends to be very, very far from optimal, and we're using #2 as baseline, so it's easy to score 100% on an given environment. You don't need anywhere close to optimality for that.

I'm sure any 2-sigma human would score near 100%, certainly above 90%. We'll try to test this hypothesis at some point.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet discusses human performance baseline on ARC-AGI-3 games, claiming a 2-sigma human would score near 100%, and that even the #1 human tester replays are far from optimal.
items.reasoning: Technical details about ARC-AGI-3 human baselines from the creator
items.summary_html: Chollet discusses human performance baseline on ARC-AGI-3 games, claiming a 2-sigma human would score near 100%, and that even the #1 human tester replays are far from optimal.
items.content_html:
@FakePsyho Having played all of these games, I feel strongly that I would have scored >95% in a real testing session. Even the #1 human tester replay tends to be very, very far from optimal, and we're using #2 as baseline, so it's easy to score 100% on an given environment. You don't need anywhere close to optimality for that.

I'm sure any 2-sigma human would score near 100%, certainly above 90%. We'll try to test this hypothesis at some point.

items.title:
Someone open-sourced a survival computer that works even when the internet goes down. 

Project N.O....
items.content:
Someone open-sourced a survival computer that works even when the internet goes down. 

Project N.O.M.A.D turns any Linux machine into a fully offline knowledge server. 

No internet needed after setup. 

The AI runs through Ollama. You can chat, write, and code locally. 

Upload your own documents and search them with semantic search. 

Everything runs in Docker containers, managed through a browser:

> Local AI with document search
> Offline Wikipedia and reference books
> Offline maps via OpenStreetMap 
> Khan Academy with progress tracking
> Encryption and data analysis tools 

One terminal command installs everything. 

It just works offline.

No cloud, no accounts, no data sent anywhere.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI describes Project N.O.M.A.D, an open-source offline survival computer turning any Linux machine into a fully offline knowledge server with local AI (Ollama), offline Wikipedia, maps, Khan Academy, and encryption tools.
items.reasoning: Interesting open-source project for fully offline AI capabilities. Practical for disaster preparedness and privacy-focused use cases. Low engagement but technically interesting.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignalAI describes Project N.O.M.A.D, an open-source offline survival computer turning any Linux machine into a fully offline knowledge server with local AI (Ollama), offline Wikipedia, maps, Khan Academy, and encryption tools.
items.content_html:
Someone open-sourced a survival computer that works even when the internet goes down.

Project N.O.M.A.D turns any Linux machine into a fully offline knowledge server.

No internet needed after setup.

The AI runs through Ollama. You can chat, write, and code locally.

Upload your own documents and search them with semantic search.

Everything runs in Docker containers, managed through a browser:

> Local AI with document search

> Offline Wikipedia and reference books

> Offline maps via OpenStreetMap

> Khan Academy with progress tracking

> Encryption and data analysis tools

One terminal command installs everything.

It just works offline.

No cloud, no accounts, no data sent anywhere.

items.title:
Vibe-coding is not about "using AI to write code".

The way I understand vibe-coding:

• You don't c...
items.content:
Vibe-coding is not about "using AI to write code".

The way I understand vibe-coding:

• You don't care about the code
• You don't review it, you don't test it
• You don't care about security
• You don't care about technical debt
• You don't care about good engineering practices

Your only focus is the final product.

I think there's a time and a place for everything. Vibe-coding is very useful, but it can also be very damaging.

items.title:
@EsamCovers Appreciate the feedback! 

If you are an early follower, I doubt you are a vibe-coder (s...
items.content:
@EsamCovers Appreciate the feedback! 

If you are an early follower, I doubt you are a vibe-coder (someone who is mass-producing code without ever looking at it and disregarding good practices).

I've noticed that many people think that vibe-coding is just using AI to write code, which is not the way I understand the term.

That being said, I hear you. I try to post a lot of useful content every day, precisely as you suggest. I should do more of that and less mocking.

thanks

Before, you needed a bad programmer to write and deploy crappy cod...
items.content:
@cubesol_greg Here is the issue:

Before, you needed a bad programmer to write and deploy crappy code.

Now, you can generate crappy code at scale without boundaries.

It's not that the quality is worse today than it was yesterday; the issue is the quantity.

items.title: 🏆The Kaggle Book — Master Data Analysis and #DataScience Competitions with #MachineLearning, GenAI, ...
items.content:
🏆The Kaggle Book — Master Data Analysis and #DataScience Competitions with #MachineLearning, GenAI, and LLMs [2nd Edition]: https://t.co/hrVstwy4zH v/ @PacktDataML

Table of Contents:
🔶Introducing Data Science Competition
🔷Organizing Data with Datasets
🔶Work & Learn with Kaggle Notebooks
🔷Kaggle Models
🔶Leveraging Discussion Forums
🔷Detailing Competition Tasks & Metrics
🔶Designing Good Validation Schemes
🔷Modeling for Tabular Competitions
🔶Hyperparameter Optimization
🔷Ensembling & Stacking Solutions
🔶Modeling Image Classification & Segmentation

My Review (on Amazon):
This 700-page masterpiece of writing covers everything you need—start to finish—to be a competitive coder, specifically for Kaggle data science competitions. The book covers the mechanics of the competitions (platform, resources, rankings, leaderboards), then the infrastructure (notebooks, GitHub, data sets, frameworks, discussion forums), and then nearly 500 pages devoted to "Elevating Your Game" (in-depth coverage of modeling techniques, evaluation metrics, validation strategies, hyperparameter optimization, ensembles, stacking, and various categories of competitions: tabular data, computer vision, NLP, Gen AI, simulations). The book concludes with a valuable section on building your Kaggle portfolio for career advancement and new opportunities. This is an outstanding data science / AI / Machine Learning training resource for anyone, even if you are not into the competitions, though especially if you are a dedicated Kaggler.

items.title: Get "Mathematics of Machine Learning" here: https://t.co/07exFk5LqL by @TivadarDanka v/ @PacktDataML...
items.content:
Get "Mathematics of Machine Learning" here: https://t.co/07exFk5LqL by @TivadarDanka v/ @PacktDataML 
—
GitHub: https://t.co/2ENjzhr35C
—
Here is my review:

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗲𝘁 𝗢𝗳 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗹𝗴𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗺𝘀 𝗧𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻 𝗙𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲

This massive book is incredible, with its comprehensive coverage of numerous fields of mathematics and their intersection with the world of AI, data science, and machine learning (AI+DSML). I remember the very first time that I encountered machine learning. This was 20+ years ago, and that was already after 20+ years of being drenched in advanced mathematics as an astrophysicist. 

That first encounter of mine with ML was this definition: "Machine learning is the set of mathematical algorithms that learn from experience" (slightly paraphrased from the original quote by Tom Mitchell, CMU). That definition surprised me, confused me, motivated me, and changed the course of my career from astrophysics into AI+DSML. 

This book by Tivadar Danka captures the full meaning of that definition. The book covers thoroughly the many areas and domains of mathematics through which patterns in data are detected, described, learned, and recognized - all for the benefit of powering ML and AI algorithms, applications, and aspirations. 

This book will motivate you, surprise you, and inspire you in many ways, no matter what level of mathematics has (or has not) already propelled your career journey. There is room for all of us to grow. 

This is a great book, worthy to sit on everyone's desktop, ready to help you explore and exploit the full set of mathematical algorithms that learn from experience. 

The book is accompanied by a rich GitHub code repository of Jupyter notebooks. Learn by doing! Do by learning!

Disclosure: the publisher provided me with a free review copy of the book.

items.title: To wit: observability tells you what your agent did, evals tell you whether it should have done that...
items.content: To wit: observability tells you what your agent did, evals tell you whether it should have done that, and you absolutely need both.

items.title: The bad news is that evaluation hasn’t caught up yet, so now people have perfect traces of systems t...
items.content: The bad news is that evaluation hasn’t caught up yet, so now people have perfect traces of systems that consistently produce wrong answers. There’s limited utility to having excellent documentation of your failures; it’s capturing every bad completion without a way to correct it.
items.author: @jason_lopatecki
items.summary: Noting that while teams now trace agents well, evaluation hasn't caught up - resulting in 'perfect documentation of failures' without correction mechanisms
items.reasoning: Identifies a real gap in the AI agent development lifecycle
items.summary_html: Noting that while teams now trace agents well, evaluation hasn't caught up - resulting in 'perfect documentation of failures' without correction mechanisms
items.content_html: The bad news is that evaluation hasn’t caught up yet, so now people have perfect traces of systems that consistently produce wrong answers. There’s limited utility to having excellent documentation of your failures; it’s capturing every bad completion without a way to correct it.

items.title: XGBoost for Regression, Predictive Modeling, and Time Series Analysis — Learn how to build, evaluate...
items.content:
XGBoost for Regression, Predictive Modeling, and Time Series Analysis — Learn how to build, evaluate, & deploy predictive models:
https://t.co/DsGwnEX177 v/ @PacktDataML
—
My review:

🟠XGBoost is definitely the focal point and central contribution of this book, along with all those other things: Regression Trees, Predictive Modeling, Time Series Analysis, and Forecasting. 

🟠This book’s added value comes in its thorough progression from start to finish with comprehensive coverage of all the major steps and issues in data science and machine learning modeling: data cleaning, imbalanced data, feature engineering, feature engineering pipeline, feature importance, categorical data encoding, model interpretability and explainability, ensembles, model training, model evaluation metrics, hyperparameter tuning, model deployment, and more - including a quick-start case study starter project that feeds into next-level projects. 

🟠I highly recommend this broadly useful book for all levels of practitioners.

items.title: A huge part of getting good at anything is simply making a lot of stuff. I mean volume. Repetition. ...
items.content:
A huge part of getting good at anything is simply making a lot of stuff. I mean volume. Repetition. Doing things over and over again.

Especially when you’re starting out, you think the people who are good must have found some secret. Like they’re more talented, or more confident, or they know something you don’t. But usually, what they’ve really done is make far more work than you realize. Probably things no one has ever seen. They might not even want to share it because it's bad. They’ve gone through draft after draft, project after project, attempt after attempt. They’ve made enough things to get strong.

That’s the most underestimated aspect of greatness. Quantity leads to quality. You do a large body of work, and inside that body of work, you begin to notice things. You notice your habits. You notice your weaknesses. You notice what keeps failing, what keeps working. You cannot learn those lessons just by thinking about the work. You only learn them by making the work.

And a lot of people quit too early. They make a few things, maybe even a few dozen things, and because the work doesn’t yet look the way they want it to, they decide they’re not good enough. But that’s not what’s happening. What’s happening is that they’re still in the process. Trust the process. More than anything else, especially when you suck and it’s painful.

You have to give yourself permission to make a lot. To make imperfect things. Bad things. Things you feel ashamed to show. Because every finished piece is teaching you something. Every attempt is building judgment. The people who get good are very often just the people who stay in the game long enough to let the process work on them.

Keep producing. Make stuff. Many stuff. A lot of stuff. Put yourself on a rhythm if you can. The path is to work. Do more. Finish more. Learn more. Trust that you arrive at quality through quantity.

items.title: 💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for #MachineLearning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]:...
items.content:
💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for #MachineLearning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]: https://t.co/Z32D8jOzxC v/ @PacktDataML

𝓒𝓸𝓷𝓽𝓮𝓷𝓽𝓼:
🔹Common Conventions & API Elements of Scikit-Learn
🔹Pre-Model Workflow and Data Preprocessing
🔹Dimensionality Reduction Techniques
🔹Build Models with Distance Metrics & Nearest Neighbors
🔹Linear Models and Regularization
🔹Advanced Logistic Regression and Extensions
🔹Support Vector Machines and Kernel Methods
🔹Tree-Based Algorithms and Ensemble Methods
🔹Text Processing and Multiclass Classification
🔹Clustering Techniques
🔹Novelty and Outlier Detection
🔹Cross-Validation and Model Evaluation Techniques
🔹Deploying Scikit-Learn Models in Production

My Review (on Amazon):
I love this book, not only for its amazing content, but also for its brilliant adherence to its "Cookbook" label. The table of contents looks repetitive, but that's the magic of it. Find the algorithm, method, or application that you want "to cook up," and the presentation follows a uniform format from chapter to chapter. This is an extraordinarily practical, useful, and reader-friendly style, perfect for the target audience: anyone who needs to use (or is discovering how to use) specific Scikit-learn tools for their Python-based machine learning tasks. Consequently, this is great not only for individual use but also for training workshops and other educational settings. Python code in a cookbook context has never looked so good. IMHO.

items.title:
What surprised me most:

A single product photo can now become studio-grade ad creatives in minutes....
items.content:
What surprised me most:

A single product photo can now become studio-grade ad creatives in minutes.

Not “AI-looking” assets.
But visuals that understand lighting, angles, and composition.

This is how teams go from slow, expensive production to rapid experimentation with consistent quality.

AI is not just generating visuals.
It’s evaluating creative quality.

→ Hooks
→ Layout
→ Visual hierarchy
→ Message clarity

The same decisions senior marketers make, now automated and repeatable.

That changes everything about testing and scale.

items.content:
For years, ads followed the same broken loop:

→ Shoot content
→ Edit endlessly
→ Revise with stakeholders
→ Repeat until budget or patience dies

The bottleneck was never ideas.
It was human bandwidth.

That constraint is quietly disappearing.

items.title: As someone who has become a victim of buying a @VW, which is a disappointment in terms of quality, I...
items.content:
As someone who has become a victim of buying a @VW, which is a disappointment in terms of quality, I can understand why @MercedesBenz is losing in almost all global markets.

First, they watered down the brand perception as luxury by starting to sell small cars competing with the Honda Civic and Toyota Yaris and then the high cost of energy due to woke European leadership made the not-luxury-anymore brand uncompetitive with other non-luxury brands in terms of price tag.

I grew up in the 1990s dreaming as a boy and then teenager that I would one day drive a Mercedes. Now I can buy it, but I don't consider it as something of value anymore.

https://t.co/QJoRKlZQI7
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Andriy Burkov comments on Mercedes-Benz losing global markets, attributing it to brand dilution and energy costs. Off-topic from AI/ML.

items.title: The holographic principle is the most shocking idea in modern physics that you might have come acros...
items.content:
The holographic principle is the most shocking idea in modern physics that you might have come across. Everything you experience in 3 dimensional space may actually be stored like information on a Giant two dimensional cosmic hard disk at the boundary of spacetime. Much like a 3D hologram emerging from a flat 2D credit card, all the depth, volume, width, and other features of our universe may be a projection derived from lower-dimensional data. Your life feels fully 3D yet the reality itself may work more like a rendering engine built from information written on the edge of space. The main information theory paradox that supports the holographic principle is the black hole information paradox. Another theory that supports the holographic principle comes from the Bekenstein Hawking area law. 

#BigData #Analytics #AI #MachineLearning #DataScience #IoT #IIoT #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #JavaScript #ReactJS #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode

items.title: @hornafricanguy No one is denying that. There are slave markets in Libya today for that matter. Why ...
items.content: @hornafricanguy No one is denying that. There are slave markets in Libya today for that matter. Why does anyone need to do that type of whataboutism when discussing the transatlantic slave trade? Especially a white South African man, when Ghana, a country that has a specific history with a specific type of slavery whose characteristics were different from the others, is discussing that history? Someone else can separately bring up other histories instead of using them to “whatabout” away a specific history that is being discussed.

items.title: The insanity of this entire event is that it took a decade and the reelection of Trump to reach the ...
items.content:
The insanity of this entire event is that it took a decade and the reelection of Trump to reach the conclusion that the decision to separate sports into men's and women's categories was based on the obvious difference in physical strength, not the difference in clothing.

The older I get, the more clearly I see that we have created and live in an insane, hallucinated, fake world ruled by inhuman hypocrites, approved or accepted by the majority as normal.

Indeed, ignorance of children and young people is bliss. Otherwise, they wouldn't want to do anything.

items.title:
I told you guys before Argentinean meat is one of the worst in the world

Their farms switched from ...
items.content:
I told you guys before Argentinean meat is one of the worst in the world

Their farms switched from free roaming grass fed beef to factory farmed feedlots like America

None of it is original great Argentinean meat that they were famous for 30 years ago

Uruguay still has free roaming grass fed beef

A tragedy really

Great article on this: https://t.co/C2sH3yBCRN
